You want everything you say, everything you do, meaning your demeanor, your voice, your tonalities, the way you approach the call, your confidence, how you position yourself as an expert, all designed that the gatekeeper would think that maybe he or she should put you through because you're a friend or a contemporary of the person. Now, if you're using, this is very important, if you're using a two-call system, this is gonna kill you, right? Now you gotta get through the gatekeeper twice, and this is where things become more complex. So what happens very often, this drives you crazy, is let's say you get through the gatekeeper the first time, and you have this awesome conversation with your decision-maker, and you hit it off, you love your product, get it, you qualify the guy, he's totally qualified, you send the guy some information, you schedule a call back for the next five days later, and now you get screened out, you can't get through the gatekeeper a second time, that happens all the fucking time. So what do you do then? Let's talk about that, okay? The most effective strategy I've ever used for this, and it worked so well guys, it was a game changer, was that when I would send out information, I included a gift, I gave them a free book about yay thick, the book was costing me like five or six bucks for the book, it's a big investment, right? It was called How to Make Money in Common Stocks, we start off, then I would send them a copy of Personal Investor Magazine, then I went to a magazine for a while, but I would send them something, a gift, a free gift, so when I call back the second time now, so now watch, I knew the secretary's name, I knew the owner, they already had a call, I already had said that, now there's no email back then, so we already mailed them information, but when they got their mail, rather than just being a letter, they throw out, they got a big thick FedEx package or UPS overnight priority with a big thick book inside, you're not throwing that out, you're going to open that 100% of the time, okay? And they'd open that, it'd be a big book, they'd say, oh and I have a little note, handwritten note with my confidence, join, let's hold this speaking to it a few days, okay? Now, when I called back a week later, I'd say, hey, it's John there, who's calling on? It's Jordan from Straton Oakmont, I don't remember the guy who said in the book last week, and they're like, and now she's like, oh, hold on a second, now they feel bad, she feels bad, because she's the one that opened up the thing, she saw the book, that's a gift of $7, that means something, remember the golden rule, do what the others do to you, right? Someone gives you a gift, what do you do? You don't talk, you don't say thank you, they feel obligated now to get on the phone with you a second time. Try this, try sending out some gift, because if you're in an industry where the clients are worth enough money, so there's always a cost, remember, it's always a cost benefit ratio, right? If you're in an industry where there's enough money to be made by getting through and you get the first call done, instead of just sending an email, like everyone else does, send them an email plus an overnight package with something in it that's thick enough or big enough that plop, it sits on their desk, and when you call back a second time and you say, hey, I'm doing, I sent that copy of XYZ Magazine or whatever it was you sent them a free report on this, your rate of getting through the second time goes up by 300%. It's not even close. I will tell you that before I started using the book, it was like literally one, I think I think one, it was like one fifth of the closing rate once we started using the book to get through the gatekeeper a second time. So that's called using an ethical bribe to get past the gatekeeper and getting on your second call. So for two call systems, remember, it's not always about just sending out an email and calling back, test out the efficacy of sending them hard copies, something that actually shows up in their office on their desk, they're going to feel, I'm not saying they're not going to buy from you because of the gift, but they'll feel obligated to speak to you a second time. And remember that falls into this whole category of more effective strategies for high value clients. I never want you to forget that at the end of the day, the laser guided making three to 400 calls a day, 500 calls a day, there's just, there's no comparison for just going plowing through, getting through as many people as you can. If you have a dialer or a system that's managing your leads, a CRM, where you could have leads that don't, if you haven't gotten through a gatekeeper, then just keep, have it insert a different time of the day, make it a nighttime call, a morning call, that's your quickest way to get through gatekeepers. I mean, if you're trying to close someone online, you're going to have to write the price down so they have to buy it. If you're looking to create leads online, no, you wouldn't have to say the price. You could just talk about the value. Remember this, okay? The big mistake people make when they sell off it is they try to try to equate the service they give or the time spent giving that service to some value like that. Let's say I'm a consultant and I spend one hour working on your company. How much is that hour worth? I mean, how much is my time worth? That's one way to look at it. Or how much money can you make by taking my advice? Let's say I spend one hour and I give you a distinction, a strategy. It allows you to make an extra five million a year. Then what was that hour worth? It's worth probably a million bucks. So you can't trade time for money. You're never going to do very well in this world selling and pricing. If you try to sell you a product based on just like its price for time, it's about how much money you're saving, what the outcome you're giving. I wouldn't post it online to price unless you're trying to close them online. So, you know, you know, if you hit, so I'm saying hit their, their personal stuff as amazing effectiveness. Right? All right. All right. Next one comes in from Devin Michael Day. Hey, Jordan, I'm building a door to door team at HT cable and internet sales. I want to build a door to our millionaires club as well. What kind of system would you build to do create multiple people to hit multiple millions and what kind of expectations and discipline is required? Listen, Nick, you know, the key, I mean, I just spoke to the door to door conference recently. It was great. There's a great group of people here. And the key to building effective teams is really at that very, very close to supervised management. In other words, you want to have people that you want, you don't want to let your salesman go run wild in the field. You know, I've seen people that go as far as where like in the solar industry or at a company I train where they actually had drivers in vans taking salespeople to certain neighborhoods and they have door knockers and they were really supervising at a that level and they let the cream rise to the top. So they had door knockers and then those people would set up a point for the closes and so on. Right. And you know, how do you do anything? It's very simple. You know, you take, you let, you have to, when you're trying to build a millionaires, you know, group, you got to do it based on egalitarian means, you know, it's got to be a meritocracy, the cream rises to the top, the people that perform get more support. They get people working for them and slowly, but sure, you build this infrastructure up where these people become managers and that's a key. Now, one important thing here, when the one mistake I see people make a lot when they're trying to build the sales force is they take the top sales person and they move them into becoming a sales manager. And what typically happens is a very, it's a very dicey business because a salesman makes the most money when they sell. I mean, you turn it into a manager, they typically stop selling and they make a lot less. So be very careful about on when you promote salespeople, don't necessarily promote them into becoming managers. You might promote them by just giving them more people to work with them to let them do the more mundane tests like cold corn, regeneration paperwork. So you support your top salespeople by allowing them to leverage off your infrastructure, all they have to do is sell. Don't necessarily promote them sideways into other jobs managing because that's not going to be in your best interest most of the time. And just remember, supervise your people closely, break them up into teams. If you want to build million dollar guys who have us being very close support, don't let them all run wild in the field. It's very difficult to run a large door to door organization without close supervision. And also give meetings every day, every day they have to have a meeting in the morning, right? Put them on the, if they put them, every person should be on the daily motivations perfectly designed for that. Every day they've got to be anchored. They should do all beyond the daily motivation with me. And then after it's done, you should give them a quick five minutes into what your interpretation was and how it applies to your industry. You do that, you'll be fucking golden. I'm Mateo Miller ass. And this is from the newsfeed guys. This was a great question. And I told them we'd respond to it. I got there are five locks to pick product myself, my company, the action threshold and the pain threshold. I'm good at the first three, but I'm a little confused on what exactly is supposed to happen or be said in the action threshold. Like, what does that really mean? I think I know what the pain threshold is all about. Imagine how it's going to be without me as your teacher. How has your guitar playing been so far without a teacher, et cetera, adding pain is easy. So he's really asking what's the action threshold and how do I, what does that really mean? So, okay. So I'll answer your question. And I also suggest that you go into the search bar and punch an action threshold. And you see a lot of different trainings. I've had some really, really great ones. I'm explaining it like 20 different ways and I'll continue explaining it 20 more different ways, because each time I explain it's a little bit different and maybe it's a little button, right? And the stuff never gets old. It's needs to always be reinforced. In short, an action threshold represents the sum of someone's buying beliefs, the beliefs they have about buying, about making decisions, about trusting people, trusting salespeople. And it's essentially how they view the world. All of those belief systems wrap up into one cohesive package that results in them landing at a certain spot in terms of how easy are they to sell to. In other words, let's be logical. If someone has a set of beliefs that say, my best decisions are made slowly, I shouldn't trust salespeople. They're mostly out to rip me off. I think I should always check with other people before I make any decision. If those are like their beliefs they have, that's going to be someone that's very, very tough to sell to. Most companies are out to get me. Most of the time people give me shitty customer service. Those are all beliefs that are going to lead someone to make very slow decisions and also have to be at a level of absolute certainty on all three 10s before they have any shot of buying. So in other words, I always say, I use the example of my father and me, right? I'm a very easy person to sell to. I have a very low action threshold, meaning the level of certain, the collective level of certainty for the three 10s. So how much I have to love a product? How much I have to trust a salesperson presenting the product? How much I have to trust to get the company standing behind that product? Those three things, well, for me, I don't have to be at a 10, 10, 10. If I'm at an eight, I'm really at, seems like a good product. Seems salesman seems like a nice guy. Company seems okay. I'm like a 788. I might buy, especially if there's a little bit of pain I'm feeling or it's not that expensive. In other words, if the energy in is not that high, money, right? Conversely, someone like my father, who's very, very tough to sell to is already a high action threshold, you know, he's got to be at a 10, 10, 10 and damn sure of it. You know, why? Because he has beliefs that say I don't trust salespeople. I got to think things over. I got to do my research. So it's my best decision to make very, very slowly to get it. Now those, now that's pretty logical, right? I don't need to take that further, right? But now let's talk about what that really translates into. So someone with those sets of beliefs about the slow decision making, not trusting, they make a decision in one way and people like me, who are very trusting and basically you don't have to, you know, you have my best to make quickly and you know, that's called low-action, right? I, my decision is made another way. Let me explain the difference. When I'm about to make a decision, I run two movies, parallel movies. I always say what's my upside if this product turns out to be as good as the salesman says, what's my life going to look like after I buy? What's my upside? How's it going to improve my life? And I'll always also run the downside. And if the sales is a lying, feeding piece of shit and he rips me off, how much is it going to negatively impact my life? The instant I'm presented with a buying op, they say, you know, in the S for the order, my brain goes, and instantly projects forward and I analyze those things that happen so fast. It's like a pentamillion, a millionth of a second. That's how fast it happens on the reporter of a second, right? Now, when I do that, I run both of those movies. I run the upside movie and I run the downside movie. When my father makes a decision, he does the same thing. He runs an upside movie and he runs a downside movie. The difference is, is that my father will run a really long-winded, very clear, very colorful downside movie. He actually thinks out the negative because all he projects it out and before he's done, his mind, his brain has him making the decision. If he does this, he'll be in the worst shape ever. It'll destroy his life. He'll be embarrassed. He'll feel terrible. His wife will hate him. He'll take up room in his closet. He'll probably steal his credit confirmation. He'll not return it. He'll be embarrassed in front of his neighbors. His life's going to be destroyed. It'll make his problem worse. Before he's done, this decision has become the worst fucking decision he ever made in his life. Then it comes to running the positive movie, meaning that if he bought it and it worked, would it improve his life? He's like, eh, maybe. He runs it, but not that he blunts the movie. He runs an exaggerated bad movie and a very blunt good movie. He's like, eh, maybe I see it. I'm like, well, it'll work. Me on the other hand, I do the opposite. I run a really, really long positive movie and I run a very short negative movie. So obviously, if someone's running a really long, toxic negative movie and they get to buy, no, they're probably not going to buy. And if someone does the opposite with a positive movie, no negative, they're probably going to buy it. So that's really what's going on with someone's action threshold. That's what's going on clinically, so to speak, right? The way we are able to then circumvent that is by using the power of language, we hijack their strategy. So we know they're running these two movies. Everybody is. So rather than letting someone like my father run this ridiculous, very long-winded movie and very short and positive one, I take control of his movie by, after I run my next loop, right? So rather than going into a closing, I'll say, you know, Max, let me ask you an honest question. What's the worst that can possibly happen here? Let's say I'm wrong. Let's say the product doesn't do what I say. The sun doesn't rise in the east set in the west. And in a course of $49,000 or you have to cash in your guarantee, you're not going to lose the pace. You're not going to destroy your life. Now, will it? And he'll say, no, yeah, not such true. He'll grudgingly admit I'm right. I said, and on the upside, if I'm right, if it works even half as good as that, yeah, you'll make some money, but it's not going to make you the richest person in town either. Correctly, yeah, true. But what it will do is serve as a benchmark for future business and what life can do for you over the long run. And now what I'll do is I'll start future pacing him and I'll make him run a long positive movie. I run it for him. I explain what his life is going to look like after he buys my practice. He's never done this. He doesn't do this. He stops it. So I stop his negative. I make sure his negative movie is what it really should be. The truth is, it's really no risk. You have a guarantee there's no risk at all. And on the upside, what I could do for you, not just with this property over the long term, with so that it can be in your life that you can trust that he, and I start running this amazing movie and what it does, it lowers his action threshold drastically just for a few minutes. And I step through that window and let me tell you something. And I asked for the order. Once you close some of the really high action threshold, and this works like a charm for those people, they make the best customers ever because once you got them, they never leave you. They don't mind paying top dollar because they just want to be taking care of what's on they can trust. And that's that. That's action threshold in a nutshell. Go look to the old trains there. You can brush up on it and you'll be great. All right. The next one comes in from Jennifer Morino.
